California
Fair POlitical
Practices Cornmission
November 2■
, ■990
Anne K. Mester
Public Utilities 
Counsel- Iv
State of California
Public Utilities 
Cornmission
5o5 Van Ness Avenue
San Francisco, cA 94LO2-3294
Re: Your Request For Advice
our File No. A-90-389
Fo 11ow-UP
Dear Ms. Mester:
This is to advise you that, upon consideration by the
Commission at its September 5, L99o meetj'ng, the advice we gave to
you in the Mester Advice Letter, No. A-9O-389, dated July 17,
l-990, has been revised.
In your letter to the commission, you sought advice-as to
whether ln item received by each nember of the California Public
Utilities 
comrnission (the ncPUc") constituted 6 ttgiftrr, or, in the
alternative, fell under the "inforrnational materialsrr exception to
the definition of gift under the Act. 
(Section a2o2a. ) The item
involved \^/as a scale nodel of a parabolic trough design solar
electric power plant collector pfate. It had been given to the
cpUC Cornrnissionlrs by a small power producer, Luz Development and
Fj-nance corporation (ttluz"), also known as Luz International'
Linited. 
we inforrned you that the item was a gift'
The Cornmissj.on considered the appeal that was filed by your
office on behalf of the cPUc comnission, and the oral- and written
conments of interested persons who contended that the scale model
was incorrectly charactlrized as a gift. 
Based on the evidence in
the adrninistralive record before it, the Comrnission deterrnined
that the scale model in questj-on should not be deemed a gift to
each of the CpUC comnissioners but, rather, should be classified
as rrinformational materials'. which are not gifts under the Act.
(Section 8202e (b) (1) . )
428 J Street, Suite 8O0

Our File No. 90-389
Page 2
Please contact me at (916) 322-590]- if you have any further
questions regarding this advice.
S incere 1y,
scott Hallabrin

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
」uly ■7′  ■990
Anne K. Mester
Public Utilities 
counsel IV
state of California
Public utilities 
commiss ion
505 van Ness Avenue
san Francisco, CA 94102-3294
Re: Your Request for Advice
our I'iIe No. A-90-389
Dear Ms. Mester:
This is in response to your request for advice on behalf of
members of the CaLifornia Public Utilities 
Commission, President
G, Mitchell wilk, comnissioner Stanley Hulett, Commissioner
Patricia Eckert, Commissioner Frederick Duda, and Commissioner
John Ohanian, concerning their duties under the conflict-of-
j.nterest provj-sions of ine Political 
Reform Act ('tthe Act").1/
OUESTTON
Does a scale model of a parabolic trough design solar
electric power plant collector p1ate, which was provided to each
member of the californj-a Public utilj.ty conmission by a srnal1
power producer, constitute a gift to each commissioner under the
Act?
. 
CONCLUSION
The scale models are gj.fts to the commissioners and do not
faLl under the 'rinformationaL naterialsrr exception to the
definition of gift under the Act.
l/ 
Governnent Code Sections 81o00-91015. ALI statutory
references are to the covernment Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at'2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq, A11 references to regulations are to
Title 2, Divlsi,on 6 of the california Code of Regulations.
428 J Street,Suite 800 0 P.0.Box 807 0 Sacramento CA 95804´ 0807 0 (916)322‐ 5660


Our Fi■e NO. A-90-389
Page 2
FACTS
on or about January 2, f99O, Luz Devel-opment and Finance
corporation (trluzrr), a snalI po$/er producer, gave to each member
of the california Public Utilities 
Commission ('rCPUCtt) a scale
model of a paraboliq.trough design solar electric power Plant
collector plate.zl 3/ 
Eaah nodel had a name plate with one of the
commi-ssioner's names on it. 
Enclosed with each model was a sheet
explaining how the Luz system (a parabolic system which transforms
solar energy to electrj.city), 
as exemplif j-ed by the mode1, worked.
several proceedings before the cPUc in which Luz is ej-ther
directly or indirectly involved may be ready for cPUc's action in
the nexl several months.4/ These matters were pending at the time
that Luz gave the rnodels in question. However, because the
comrnissioners only recently learned that the cost of the nodel was
over $30o, did the need arise for clarification 
of the
characterization of the model- under the Act.
The commissioners contend that the models are educationaf
devices r^/hich they use to demonstrate to foreign visitors and
other interested persons state of the art technoloqy in the
production of solar thermal electricity. 
Further, they argue that
the models have no personal value to then in terms of personal use
or i-n terrns of resal-e.
ANALYS I S
Section 82028(a) defines u ttgiftr as any pa)rrnent to the
extent that consideration of equaf or greater value is not
2/ Luz al-so is a lobbyist employer under the Act. 
(section
82039.5.)
rl 
Luz aLso has qiven the solar col-lector model in question to
members of other regulatory agencies, to senior administrative and
executive officials with existj,ng and prospective customers
(utility 
companies), and to various designated individuals at
business entities, 
such- as law, engineering, and finance rnarketing
firms, who work with Luz on j.ts projects on a regular basis.
*t 
In your letter to the Commission you stated that Luz was a
party to pending proceedings. Hor^rever, j.n subsequent telephone
conversations, you advised us that Luz r"ras not a party to
proceedings, but rather a direct beneficiary of the decisions. In
tnis regaid, you indicitea that southern caLifornia Edison is
seeking approval of a transmission line. 
on behalf of Southern
California Edison, Luz is a provider of the power that will be
running through the proposed transmission Ii.ne.
on Juty 10, 1990, the commission Learned for the first 
time
that a pending decision in this matter was scheduled to be heard
on July 18, 1990.


Our Fi■e No. A-90-389
Page 3
received. section 82044 defines rrpaymentrr as a pa)ment,
distribution, transfer, 1oan, advance, deposit, gift or other
rendering of money, property, services or anything of vafue,
whether tangible or intangible.
certai.n types of pa)rments are excluded fron the definition of
ttglftrt. 
Specif ical-l-y, Section 82028(b) provides that the tern
ngiftt! does not include:
(1) Informational material such as books,
reports, panphlets, calendars or periodicals. 
No
paynent for travel or reimbursenent for any
expenses shall- be deemed "informational material.tt
(2) Gifts which are not used and which,
within 30 days after receipt, are returned to the
donor or delivered to a charitable organization
without being claimed as a charitable contribution
for tax PurPoses.
***
(6) Personalized plaques and trophies with an
individuaL value of less than two hundred fifty
dolLars ($250).
(section 82028 (b) (L) , (2), (6) . )
Under.this definition anvthinq of value qiven to a public
official 
without cost is a gift and must be reported.unless
expr"=sfy exempted. (Section 82028. ) Free inforrnational
materiatl such as reports or periodicals, are expressly exernpted
from the rrgiftn definition. 
Although Section 82028 contains no
specific reference to intangible informational material, the
clrnrnission has held that t,11e exception for informational rnaterial-
may appl-y, under certain circumstances, to an intangible such as
an- iniorlnational tour. 
(In re sDellman, 1 FPPC ops. 16; accord
Leidiqh Advice Letter, No. A-89-248; but see, glEl]l Advice Letter,
tlo. eles-zrg and the aquilar Advice Letter, No. A-86-243, copies
enclosed. )
In your letter, you state that because the rnodels have no
value to the commj.ssioners in terrns of personal use or ln terms of
resale, you do not believe they should be considered as giftstl
under ieition 
82028 (a) . Past Conmission advice on this issue does
not accept this interpretation. 
The commission has, over the
y"u.., rlceived :nany iiiquiries concerning gifts to pubtic
-otti"i.l=. 
Many such gifts ,"re unique and not sold conmercially.
(corv Advice Letter, No. 75-116,' copy enclosed') In some
iiEii"..r 
the official 
derived little 
or no benefit frorn the gift.
(In re Hopkins 3 FPPC ops. 1o?.) Nevertheless, the Cornmission has
i"""d--that- th"se iterns have va lue and are deemed gif ts to the
officials.


Our Fi■e No. A-90-389
Page 4
You also urge the Commission to adopt the 'r j-nformational
naterialrr exception. You liken the scale model of a parabolic
trough design solar electric power plant collector plate to a tour
of the facility 
where the actual solar collector j-s located and in
operation. The comnission does not believe the personalized solar
collector plate modeL .in question is Ij.ke the tours whj-ch we have
found to be informational material exceptions under the Act. In
those particular sj.tuations, the Commission found that the benefit
fl-owed to the governmental agency and not to the individual who,
under those particular circunstances, was merely acting as the
conduit of the inforrnation on behalf of his or her agency.
The general rule is that public offj,cial,s nust report gifts
of $50 or more. Exceptions to this general rule must be narrowly
construed. (See Estate of Baneriee (L978), 2L cal,. 3d 527, 54o.)
The model herein was given to each commissioner individually with
his or her personally j,nscribed naneplate. It was given to each
Comnissioner by a lobbyist employer at a time vJhen a matter was
pending before the cPUc in which the lobbyist enployer had a
iinancial interest. 
The fact that the model may have been given
by Luz for demonstration purposes does not alter the status of the
nodel from a gift to a informational tour or to any of the other
expressly provided exceptions under the Act. af L:uz had wanted to
provide CPUC \,{ith a model for demonstration (infornational)
purposes, we believe it could have done so in a manner that would
not have triggered the Act/s disclosure and disqual if ication
provisions. 
(Ke1lv Advice Letter, No. A-89-458, copy enclosed. )
Since. each of the commissioners has accepted the nodel $iith
his or her name inscribed thereon without returning lhe model to
the donor within the statutorily prescribed 3o days,5/ and since
the cost of the model exceeds $50, they must report the model as a
gift from Luz Developnent and Finance corporat.ion on their
itatenents of economic interests. 
(section 872o7 (a) (L) -)
Moreover, since the value of the model exceeds $250, the
commissioners al.so nay be required to disqualify thernselves for
12 months after receipt from the pending decision which will have
5/ In a subsequent Letter dated June 11, 1990, you advised us
that one of the commissioners, Patricia Eckert, did not actually
take possession of the model. It was received by her office staff
when -she was out of totqn.' one of her advisors opened the packaqe,
found the nodel to be broken, and threw it away. Luz $/as advised
of this action and never provided Commj.ssioner Eckert with a
substitute nodeI. Discarding or otherwise turning a gift over to
another person (other than as provided in subsection (b) of
Regulatiln L8'726.1, copy enclosed) constitutes acceptance of the
giit Uy the official. 
(See also Thornas Advice Letter, No. I-89-
3 61, copY enclosed. )


Our Fi■e No. A-90-389
Page 5
a reasonably foreseeable rnaterial financiaL effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on:
Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for
a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating two hundred
fifty 
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by, or prornised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the tirne when the
decision is made.
(section 87103 (e) . )
whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the tirae a governnental decision is made
depends on the facts of each case. An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that
it lriIl occur. Certainty is not requj-red. However' if an effect
is only a mere possibility, 
it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In
re Thorner, 1 FPPC ops. 198, copy encLosed.) Based on the facts
provided, it appears reasonably foreseeable that the decision
iegardj-ng the approval of southern cal,ifornia Edison's
transrnission line will have an econornic effect on Luz.
In addition, the foreseeable effect on the donor also nust be
material- to requi,re disqualification. 
The commission has adopted
differing guidelines to determine lthether an effect is material,
depending on the specific circurnstances of each decision. For
exlmple, \,rhere a donor is directly before the official's 
agency,
Regu1ation.L8702. 1 (copy enclosed) provides that the effect of the
deiision on the donor is deemed nateri.al and disqual if ication is
required. 
(lilarknan Advice Letter, No. A-89-172, copy enclosed.)
A party is directty involved in a proceeding when the party,
either personally or by an agent:
(1) Initiates t+re proceeding in which the
decision wil"1 be nade by filing an application,
claim, appeal, or similar request or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject
of, the proceeding- concerning the decision before
the official 
or the official's 
agency.
(3) A person or business entity is the
subject of a proceeding if a decis j.on involves the
issuance, rener^/al, approval, denial or revocation
of any license, permit, or other entitlernent to, or
contract with, the'S.ubject person or business
entity.
(Regulation 
■8702.■(b)。
)


Our Fi■e No. A-90-389
Page 6
where a donor, which is a business entity, is not directly
before the official's 
agency, Regulation 7a702.2 appIies.6/
whether the indirect effect on a business entity is material
depends on tfre financial size of the business entity involved. 
we
do not have sufficient information about the financial size of
Luz. A copy of Regulation l-8702.2 is enclosed to enable you to
determine whether a decision will have a material financial effect
on Luz.
Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision is ruaterial, disqual ification is required only if the
effect is distinguishabl-e from the effect on the public generally.
(Section a7Lo3. ) ff the decision does not affect all the members
of the public in the same manner, disqual j.f ication may be required
unless the effect of the decision on the donor is the same as the
effect on a significant segment of the public. 
(ReguLation L8703,
copy enclosed. )
In sum, given the j.nfornation presented to the Commission, it
appears reasonably foreseeabl,e that the pending decisions,
including the decj-slon of JuIy 18 will have a financial effect on
Luz, and such effect is distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally. ff this financial effect is rnaterial as set
forth in either Regulations L8702.1 or 18702.2, then there will be
a confLict of interest and the cornmissioners will-be required to
disqualify themselves from acting on this matter. //
6/ There is some confusion frorn the facts provided !'/hether Luz is
directly, or indirectly, 
invotved in the pending decisions
referenced in your letter, or just the pending decision currently
scheduted for JuIy 18, -l-990. For that reason, an analysis of both
Regulation La702.1 and Regulation 18702.2 is presented.
7 / In the event of such disqual if ication, the tlrul-e of necessj,tyr
normally comes into p1ay. (See, I'Legal1y Required Participation
in covernnentaL Decision'r Section 87101; Regulation 18701; In re
Brown 4 FPPC Ops. 19, tropies enclosed. ) Hor,/ever, the Hopkins
opinion, supra at 10, provides that section 87101 does not apply
to a conflict that arj-ses because of gifts an official 
has
accepted if it was reasonably foreseeable at the tj.me the gift(s)
was received that the official 
would be asked to make or
participate in the making of a qovernmental decision affecting the
donor. (See aLso, White Advice Letter, No. O-8 6-00l / A-A6-f43 ,
copy enclosed. )


'   Our File No. A-90-389
Page 7
I trust that this answers your question. If you have any
further questions regardj,ng this matter, please feel free to
contact rne at (916) 322-590]--
Sincerel-y,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
易

CounseL, Legal Division
